[...] For the smallest and small primary producers from low-risk countries, a one-time registration in the", '# EU Regulation on Deforestation-free Products (EUDR)

The Regulation on Deforestation-free Products (EUDR), Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, regulates trade in certain agricultural and timber products: Import, export and trade on the EU market are only permitted if products are demonstrably deforestation-free and legal. The EU aims to minimise the part played by European consumption in global deforestation by introducing binding corporate due diligence obligations. These obligations will affect the following raw materials: wood, cocoa, coffee, palm oil, rubber, beef and soya, as well as products made from them. Violations can result in heavy fines, product confiscation and exclusion from the market. [...] The regulation requires companies to track their supply chains. The competent authority for implementation in Germany is the Federal Office for Agriculture and Food. The EUDR will replace the earlier EUTR (EU Regulation No. 995/2010, EU Timber Regulation). Special rules apply during a transitional period until 2028, for which the date of harvesting and placing on the market are decisive.

## Actors & obligations

response:
**Headline Finding** â The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR, Regulation (EU) 2023/1115) requires German timber exporters to prove that their products are deforestation-free and legally produced, with compliance enforced by the Federal Office for Agriculture and Food as of early 2026.

The EUDR covers wood and timber products, among other commodities, and mandates that companies placing, supplying, or exporting these products must demonstrate that they were not produced on land deforested after December 31, 2020, and that all relevant legal requirements in the country of production were met. Companies must submit a due diligence declaration and track their supply chains to ensure compliance. Violations can result in heavy fines, product confiscation, and exclusion from the EU market.

The regulation replaces the previous EU Timber Regulation (EUTR, No. 995/2010) and introduces binding corporate due diligence obligations. In Germany, the Federal Office for Agriculture and Food (BLE) is responsible for implementation, providing detailed guidance and resources for affected companies. The German timber trade welcomed the EUDR, noting that the obligation to submit due diligence declarations now rests solely with companies first placing products on the EU market, not downstream traders. The EU Commission is expected to present a report on administrative burdens by the end of April 2026.
reference: The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR, Regulation EU 2023/1115) requires that timber and other commodity products traded on the EU market are demonstrably deforestation-free and legally produced. Implementation has been postponed twice by the end of 2025. The German timber trade association GD Holz welcomed the revision of the EUDR, calling it a success for the entire wood value chain and stating it provides practical solutions that protect forests without overburdening companies. Companies must implement due diligence systems and submit documentation to prove compliance. The Federal Office for Agriculture and Food (BLE) is the designated German enforcement authority.

response:
**Headline Finding** â German building permits for residential construction rose sharply in January 2026, with an 8.4% year-on-year increase to 19,500 units, marking the first significant growth after several years of decline.

According to Fordaq and Finanznachrichten, permits for new-build housing in January 2026 increased by 7.4% to 16,400 units, while approvals for dwellings created through conversion of existing buildings climbed by 13.5% to 3,100 units. Across all residential building types, permits rose by 6.0% compared to January 2025. The strongest growth was seen in single-family houses, up 12.6%, and two-family houses, which jumped by 26.1%. Multi-family building permits, which make up the largest share of new housing, increased by 7.1% year-on-year.

Regionally, Hamburg saw a notable boost in permit numbers, with nearly 750 additional approvals attributed to new federal legislation known as the "Bau-Turbo," as reported by Stern and Abendblatt. This policy change has been credited by city officials with accelerating the permitting process and enabling hundreds of new housing units.

Industry sources, including the Zentralverband Deutsches Baugewerbe (ZDB) and the Deutsches Institut fÃ¼r Wirtschaftsforschung (DIW), confirm that 2026 marks a tentative turnaround for the German construction sector. DIW projects a 1.7% increase in total construction volume for 2026 after a 1.2% decline in 2025, while ZDB expects residential construction revenue to rise from â¬54 billion in 2025 to â¬56.3 billion in 2026. However, multiple sources caution that this recovery remains fragile, with risks from rising energy prices and potential interest rate hikes linked to geopolitical instability, particularly the Iran conflict.

Permits for residential construction rose 8.4% year-on-year to 19,500 units in January 2026 â making early engagement with permitting authorities and monitoring policy changes like the "Bau-Turbo" critical for project planning.
reference: German housing construction permits rose 8.4% year-on-year to 19,500 units in January 2026, the first significant increase after several years of decline. New build permits rose 7.4% to 16,400 units, conversions rose 13.5% to 3,100 units. Single family homes rose 12.6% and two-family homes rose 26.1%. The federal Bau-Turbo regulation produced nearly 750 additional building approvals in Hamburg within four months. The upward trend is at risk from the Iran war escalation which could raise energy prices and interest rates. In 2024, only 215,900 units were approved, the lowest since 2010. Construction sector forecasts for 2026 remain cautious with high material costs and skills shortages continuing.

Germanyâs wood pellet fuel market is strongly shaped by the Energiewende policy, which aggressively promotes renewable energy sources for heating and power generation. Companies such as Graanul Invest and German Pellets have contributed to a robust production base, while sustainable forestry practices ensure a steady supply of raw materials.

**Headline Finding** â European Central Bank (ECB) interest rate decisions directly affect German construction by influencing mortgage rates, which in turn impact housing demand and building activity, as seen in early 2026 when mortgage rates climbed to their highest levels since mid-2024, dampening demand.

In January 2026, German mortgage rates reached their highest point since mid-2024, which sources confirm led to reduced demand for housing loans. According to ING Think, this was a direct result of capital market movements and ECB rate expectations, with German 10-year yields rising by more than 30 basis points since late February 2026, likely pushing lending rates back above 4%. The ING analysis highlights that in 2022, a rapid series of ECB rate hikes caused mortgage rates to surge from near-zero to over 3.5%, bringing the German housing market to a near standstill. For 2026, most anticipated ECB rate hikes are already priced in, and while no further hikes are expected in the base case, any additional tightening would likely have a less severe effect than in 2022.

Building permit data from Holzkurier shows that 19,500 new housing units were approved in Germany in January 2026, an increase of 8.4%, indicating some resilience in construction activity despite higher financing costs. However, the ifo Institute reports a continued high rate of order cancellations in residential construction, reflecting ongoing sensitivity to interest rate changes and financing conditions.

**Headline Finding** â German new housing construction is set to fall below 200,000 completed units in 2026, marking a severe contraction despite a modest uptick in building permits at the start of the year.

According to reporting from the Wohnungsbau-Tag 2026 industry summit, experts expect the number of newly built homes in Germany to drop below 200,000 for the yearâless than half the volume considered necessary to meet demand. The event summary describes the housing market as "frozen," with high pressure and a lack of available units for new households, as highlighted by Arnt von Bodelschwingh of RegioKontext. The sector is described as being in a state of crisis, with the shortage of new construction directly impacting both mobility and economic strength.

Official data from the Statistisches Bundesamt (Destatis) confirms a slight improvement in early 2026 building activity: in January, 19,500 housing units were approved nationwide, an increase of 8.4% (or 1,500 units) compared to January 2025. Of these, 16,400 were new builds (+7.4% or +1,100 units year-on-year), and 3,100 resulted from conversions of existing buildings (+13.5% or +400 units). Within new construction, permits for single-family homes rose by 12.6% to 3,800 units, two-family homes by 26.1% to 1,200 units, and multi-family homes by 7.1%.

Industry commentary from the GdW housing association acknowledges this modest permit growth as a "slight revival" at the start of 2026, but notes that the increase in multi-family home permitsâcrucial for housing supplyâremains subdued at 7.1%. The association calls for improved policy conditions to address the ongoing supply crisis.

The drop to under 200,000 new completions in 2026, despite January's 8.4% year-on-year permit increase, signals that the sector remains in deep contraction and that the recent uptick in permits has not yet translated into a reversal of the broader downward trend.

**Headline Finding** â Construction costs in Germany remain elevated in early 2026, driven by energy price shocks from the Iran war, persistent inflation in building materials and finishing work, and ongoing regulatory and policy uncertainty.

According to the Statistische Bundesamt, prices for new conventionally built residential buildings in Germany rose by 3.2% year-on-year as of November 2025. The cost pressure is particularly acute in finishing work, with prices for these services up 3.8% over the same period. Heating and central water heating systems, such as heat pumps, saw the sharpest increase, rising by 4.4% year-on-year in November 2025.

Multiple sources confirm that the Iran war has introduced significant new uncertainty into the construction sector, primarily through its impact on energy prices and the risk of further interest rate hikes. Handelsblatt and ZDF report that rising energy costs and the potential for higher financing costs are major contributors to the current cost environment, with the geopolitical situation cited as a key risk factor for both material prices and project financing.

Regulatory and policy factors also play a role. While some local governments, such as Dresden's city council, have moved to simplify permitting processes in an effort to stimulate construction, experts cited in Handelsblatt warn that without broader reforms and a reduction in costly building requirements, affordable housing will remain scarce and construction costs will stay high.

Statistical data and industry reporting confirm that the combination of energy price shocks from the Iran war, inflation in key building components, and regulatory hurdles are the primary drivers of construction cost increases in Germany as of early 2026.
reference: Construction costs in Germany remain elevated in early 2026 driven by energy price shocks from the Iran war, persistent inflation in building materials and finishing work, and ongoing regulatory uncertainty. The Iran war escalation is identified as a key risk threatening to slow the fragile construction recovery. The Bundesbauministerin Hubertz promoted the idea of a federal housing construction company to address the shortage. The German construction building index reached 100 points for the first time in two years in Q4 2025. Rising interest rates for construction loans are adding further cost pressure for developers and buyers.

**Headline Finding** â German sawmills in early 2026 are facing an acute raw material shortage, with timber prices at record highs and warehouses running empty, threatening the sector with an existential crisis.

Sources confirm that German timber prices for spruce and pine have reached unprecedented levels in 2026, with the timber industry raising prices significantly at the start of the year. Forest farmers are receiving higher revenues for coniferous roundwood than ever before, but sawmills are struggling with empty warehouses and skyrocketing procurement costs. The German Federal Association of the Wood Industry describes the current situation as a âraw material shortage,â warning that if more wood does not enter the market soon, the crisis could escalate into an âexistential crisis for the sawmill and timber industry.â

The shortage is not limited to high-quality logs; lower-quality wood, industrial wood, and energy wood are also experiencing price surges due to the scarcity. Industrial wood, in particular, has seen the largest percentage price increases, as many sawmills now use their own sawmill residues for pellet production and must purchase additional industrial wood on the open market, further driving up prices.

Sawmills are also contending with weak demand from the construction sector, which remains the primary sales market for wood products. Although building permits have risen slightly, the overall level is still well below previous years, and no real trend reversal is evident. The combination of high raw material costs and subdued demand is placing additional strain on sawmill operations.

Empty warehouses are widespread, with reports from February 2026 indicating that many sawmills have âgÃ¤hnend leerâ (completely empty) inventories due to continued low deliveries of fresh logs. The Bundesverband der Holzindustrie (Federal Association of the Wood Industry) has labeled the situation as âRohstoffunterversorgungâ (raw material under-supply), reiterating the risk of an existential crisis if supply does not improve imminently.

Timber prices at record highs and acute raw material shortages make securing new log supply contracts the immediate operational priority for German sawmills.
reference: German sawmills in early 2026 face an acute raw material shortage with sawmill inventories running empty. Spruce and pine timber prices have reached record highs with the German Timber Industry Association HDH warning that if more wood does not come to market the situation will escalate into an existential crisis for the sawmill and timber industry. Sawmills are paying record prices for spruce B 2b+ at 130 to 135 euros per cubic meter yet still cannot secure sufficient supply. Lower quality wood grades including industrial wood and energy wood are also benefiting from the shortage. The market paradox is that despite weak construction demand and sluggish export markets, prices are rising due to a supply-driven shortage.

**Headline Finding** â German residential building permits rose 10.8% to 238,500 units in 2025, signaling a construction sector rebound that is directly increasing timber demand and contributing to record-high wood prices in early 2026.

According to preliminary data from Statistisches Bundesamt (Destatis), Germany approved 238,500 homes in 2025, a 10.8% increase â or 23,200 more units â compared to 2024, which had marked the lowest permit level since 2010. Within new residential buildings, 194,200 units were approved, up 13.2% year-on-year, with single-family home permits rising sharply by 17.2% to 44,500 units. This marks the first annual increase in residential building permits since 2021, and industry sources such as the German Macroeconomic Policy Institute (IMK) describe this as a strong signal of sector recovery. The rise in permits is attributed to new legislation passed in October 2025 that expedited planning and approval processes, allowing municipalities to approve projects deviating from the Building Code if they serve new residential construction.

Despite this rebound in permits, the real estate association GdW expects completions to drop to just 200,000 apartments in 2026, well below the estimated annual need of 300,000 units. Cost pressures remain acute, with the average price per square meter of new living space in major cities reaching â¬4,630, or â¬5,400 including land costs, according to Dietmar Walberg of the Arge institute. Nevertheless, improving affordability and mortgage availability, alongside government subsidy programs and efforts to reduce bureaucracy, are expected to sustain positive momentum in the housing market through 2026.

This recovery in construction activity is already impacting timber demand. The residential construction sector is the largest end-use segment for treated construction timber in Germany, and sources confirm that demand for timber is closely linked to the volume and type of construction activity. As the housing market stabilizes and previously deferred demand re-enters the market in 2026, timber demand is experiencing steady, demand-led growth. Industry forecasts project an 8.3% compound annual growth rate for the German softwood timber market from 2026 to 2033, driven by rising construction, green building initiatives, and export opportunities.

On the supply side, the surge in demand has contributed to an acute raw material shortage and record-high wood prices in early 2026. Prices for spruce and pine are higher than ever before, with sawmills facing empty warehouses and significantly increased costs. Forest owners are receiving unprecedented revenues for coniferous roundwood, and even lower-quality wood grades are benefiting from the shortage. The tight market supply and rising prices are directly linked to the rebound in construction activity and the resulting increase in timber demand.

Spruce and pine prices reached all-time highs in Q1 2026 as sawmills faced acute raw material shortages, a direct result of the sharp rebound in residential building permits and construction activity.
reference: German residential building permits rose 10.8% to 238,500 units in 2025, the first annual increase since 2021, signaling a sector turnaround. In January 2026 permits rose a further 8.4% to 19,500 units. German softwood timber market is forecast to grow at a compound annual growth rate of 8.3% from 2026 to 2033 driven by rising construction activity and government green building initiatives. German timber prices for spruce and pine reached record highs in early 2026 with sawmills facing empty warehouses. Business associations are warning of spiralling construction costs in Germany. Germany has a desperate shortage of affordable housing with demand for new builds urgently needed.

**Headline Finding** â German housing construction permits rose 8.4% year-on-year in January 2026, reaching 19,500 units, marking the first significant increase after several years of decline.

According to the Federal Statistical Office (Destatis), 19,500 housing units were approved in January 2026, an increase of 8.4% or 1,500 permits compared to January 2025. Of these, 16,400 units were new builds, up 7.4% or 1,100 units year-on-year, while conversions and extensions in existing buildings accounted for 3,100 units, a 13.5% or 400-unit rise.

The breakdown shows that permits for new single-family homes climbed 12.6% to 3,800 units, and two-family homes saw a 26.1% increase to 1,200 units. Multifamily dwellings, which represent the largest segment, also contributed to the overall growth, supporting a continued upward trend in both single- and multifamily housing approvals.

Sources note that this improvement follows a prolonged downturn in the German residential construction sector. However, several reports highlight emerging risks: the ongoing Iran conflict has led to rising energy prices and the potential for higher interest rates, both of which could dampen the positive momentum seen at the start of 2026.

Housing permits in Germany increased by 8.4% year-on-year to 19,500 units in January 2026 â the first clear sign of recovery in residential construction, but ongoing geopolitical and economic uncertainties warrant close monitoring of subsequent monthly data.

This marks the first significant increase after three consecutive years of decline. New build permits rose 7.4% to 16,400 units. Single family homes rose 12.6%, two-family homes rose 26.1%, and multi-family homes rose 7.1% to 10,500 units. The upward trend is at risk from potential interest rate increases and energy price rises resulting from the Iran war escalation. Residential property prices in Germany were also rising at the start of 2026.

**Headline Finding** â In February 2026, the United States introduced a new import surcharge and continued restrictions on low-value shipments, creating a more complex and costly environment for European timber exporters.

According to a February 2026 report by WTS Global, the U.S. implemented a series of significant trade measures affecting all exporters to the country, including European timber businesses. These changes are distinct from previous tariff escalations, as they combine a major Supreme Court ruling with a new Section 122 import surcharge and the ongoing application of restrictions on low-value shipments. The policy environment is described as more unpredictable and expensive for European exporters, with the Section 122 surcharge potentially subject to amendment, extension, or replacement before July 2026.

No source provides a specific figure for the new surcharge or quantifies its direct impact on timber prices, but the WTS Global analysis highlights the need for European businesses to consider supply chain adjustments, such as U.S. or near-shore finishing and alternative logistics models, to mitigate increased costs and disruptions. The report also notes that the policy landscape remains fluid, with further changes possible in the coming months.

Available reporting does not directly link these U.S. tariff changes to immediate price movements in European timber markets, nor does it provide evidence of a direct causal impact on European timber prices as of March 2026. However, the increased complexity and cost for exporters are expected to influence supply chain strategies and could affect market dynamics if the surcharges remain or intensify.

WTS Global confirms the Section 122 surcharge introduction in February 2026, and the potential for further policy changes before July 2026 is cited as a key factor shaping European timber exporters' strategies.

**Headline Finding** â Rising European Central Bank interest rates have pushed German mortgage rates above 4% in early 2026, dampening housing construction demand and leading to muted timber consumption.

According to ING Think, German mortgage rates climbed to their highest levels since mid-2024 in January 2026, exceeding 4% after a sharp sell-off in government bond markets triggered by Middle East tensions and rising energy prices. This increase in financing costs has directly dampened demand for new housing, with sources noting that the brief relief in February from lower capital market rates was quickly reversed. The ING analysis confirms that after a solid rebound in 2025, momentum in the German housing market softened again in early 2026 due to these interest rate pressures.

Reporting from Handelsblatt on March 18, 2026, further highlights that while there has been a recent uptick in building permits, the prospect of further ECB rate hikesâespecially in the context of geopolitical uncertaintyâremains a significant risk to sustained growth in German residential construction. The article explicitly links the threat of rising interest rates to a potential slowdown in housing demand, despite some positive signals from permit data.

On the timber consumption side, Fastmarkets' January 2026 report notes that German market activity for softwood timber remained muted, with limited demand from domestic construction. While spruce sawfalling 44-50Ã150 mm edged up by 3.17% compared to Decemberâs midpoint, other grades held flat, and Nordic suppliers reported limited exposure to the German market. This subdued timber demand is consistent with the dampened housing construction activity attributed to higher borrowing costs.

German mortgage rates exceeding 4% in early 2026âdriven by ECB policy and bond market reactionsâhave directly constrained housing construction demand, which in turn has resulted in muted timber consumption as confirmed by both construction and timber market sources.
reference: German mortgage rates climbed to their highest levels since mid-2024 in January 2026, dampening housing construction demand. The German housing market rebound is described as likely to bend but not break. German house prices rose 3.2% in 2025 but momentum has softened again in early 2026. European construction is expected to grow 2.4% in 2026 according to the EUROCONSTRUCT network. The European sawn timber market in January 2026 showed cautious demand with early signs of stabilization. Germany saw the most notable sawn timber price movement in January 2026 with spruce sawfalling edging up 3.17% while other grades held flat.

For 2b+ in B/C grades, prices are around â¬96â100 per cubic meter. Beetle-damaged wood is discounted by â¬20 per cubic meter off the BC price, but even lower-quality logs are being accepted by sawmills due to acute shortages.

# European Commission proposes adjustments to the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)

On October 22, 2025 the European Commission presented a proposal to modify the implementation timeline of the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR). According to this new proposal, large and medium-sized companies would need to comply as of December 2025, while micro and small enterprises would have an additional year, until December 2026.']
response:
**Headline Finding** â The practical application deadline for the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) has been postponed by one year to December 30, 2026, for German timber companies.

According to Holz-Zentralblatt (published January 9, 2026), all companies now have until December 30, 2026, to implement the EUDR requirements, following a one-year postponement. This extension is intended to give the EU Commission additional time to improve the IT system supporting EUDR implementation. The changes also introduce simplifications for small private forest owners in the EU, who will now only need to submit a one-time simplified declaration. Furthermore, only companies placing relevant products on the EU market for the first timeâsuch as EU forest owners and importersâare required to submit due diligence statements, while subsequent traders and marketers are exempt.

A report from PSQR (March 3, 2026) confirms that the new compliance deadlines extend into late 2026 and mid-2027, but emphasizes that operational preparations remain complex. Companies must still develop capabilities for supplier information collection, geolocation data capture, risk assessment standardization, and integration of compliance workflows. The scope of the regulation has also been narrowed, with printed products removed from coverage, focusing EUDR on core forest-risk commodities and derived goods.

CMS (January 8, 2026) notes that a six-month transition period without sanctions began in January 2026, pending final approval by the European Parliament and Council. Until the proposal is formally adopted, the original compliance start date of December 2025 technically remains in force, but the practical effect is the new December 30, 2026, deadline.

December 30, 2026, is now the operative EUDR compliance deadline for German timber companies, but ongoing legislative review and the upcoming European Commission impact report (due April 30, 2026) could further clarify or adjust requirements.
reference: The EU Deforestation Regulation application deadline was postponed by one year following a European Parliament vote of 405 to 242 on December 17, 2025, confirmed by the EU Council the next day and published in the Official Journal on December 23, 2025. Large and medium operators must comply by December 30, 2026. Small and micro enterprises have until June 30, 2027. Compliance requires transitioning from manual tracking to digital traceability infrastructure. A six-month transition period without sanctions beginning January 2026 was included in an earlier proposal. Forest owners are calling for further simplifications beyond the postponement.
